successCriteria.description: `src/shared/types/index.ts` exports enum `LeaveRequestStatus` with values: DRAFT, SUBMITTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, CANCELLED
`src/shared/types/index.ts` exports enum `EmploymentStatus` with values: ACTIVE, INACTIVE, TERMINATED
`src/shared/errors/index.ts` exports `NotFoundError` class extending Error, constructor takes `entityName: string` and `id: string`
`src/shared/errors/index.ts` exports `ValidationError` class extending Error, constructor takes `message: string` and optional `fieldErrors` map
`src/shared/errors/index.ts` exports `ConflictError` class extending Error, constructor takes `message: string`

`src/shared/db/base.repository.ts` exports abstract generic class `BaseRepository<T>` with methods: `findById(id: string): Promise<T | null>`, `findAll(filters?: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<T[]>`, `create(entity: Omit<T, 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'updatedAt'>): Promise<T>`, `update(id: string, updates: Partial<T>): Promise<T | null>`, `delete(id: string): Promise<boolean>`
`BaseRepository` wraps the pg Pool from `src/shared/db/connection.ts` and uses parameterized queries (no string concatenation of SQL)
Jest unit tests in `tests/unit/shared/` cover error classes: verify correct instanceOf, message formatting, and property assignment for NotFoundError, ValidationError, ConflictError
Jest unit tests in `tests/unit/shared/` cover BaseRepository methods with a mocked pg Pool, verifying correct SQL parameterization and return types for findById, findAll, create, update, delete

constraints: All database access must go through the repository pattern (GP-001) — BaseRepository is the foundation for this
Strict TypeScript: no implicit any, strict null checks (from tsconfig.json and HARNESS.json constraints)
Use parameterized queries via the pg Pool — no string concatenation of SQL (HARNESS constraint: no-direct-db-outside-repository, plus explicit intent requirement)
Tests must match Jest config: `**/tests/**/*.test.(ts|js)`, ts-jest preset, node environment
Modules import from each other only through declared public entry points (index.ts barrel exports)
outOfScope: Phase 7 — Database migrations for leave management tables
Phase 8 — Leave service business logic (submit, approve, reject, cancel lifecycle)
Phase 9 — Leave controller and Fastify HTTP routes
Audit logging (GP-002) — no audit infrastructure is created in this phase; that is deferred to later phases
RBAC enforcement (GP-005) — no middleware or role checks in this phase

ambiguities.description: BaseRepository is abstract and generic but the intent does not specify how it resolves the target table name. Concrete subclasses need a mechanism to provide the table name. Options: (a) abstract `tableName` property getter, (b) constructor parameter, (c) class decorator / static property.
options: Abstract property getter `protected abstract get tableName(): string`

ambiguities.description: The `create` method accepts `Omit<T, 'id' | 'createdAt' | 'updatedAt'>` and returns `Promise<T>`, implying id/createdAt/updatedAt are generated. The intent does not specify the generation strategy: UUID via `crypto.randomUUID()`? PostgreSQL `gen_random_uuid()`? `DEFAULT` clause in the schema? `NOW()` for timestamps?
options: Generate `id` via `crypto.randomUUID()` in TypeScript, `createdAt`/`updatedAt` via `new Date()`
Use PostgreSQL `RETURNING *` clause and let DB defaults handle id (uuid_generate_v4) and timestamps (NOW())
Hybrid: generate id in TS, let DB handle timestamps via DEFAULT NOW()

ambiguities.description: `findAll(filters?: Record<string, unknown>)` — the intent does not specify how filters translate to SQL. Are they AND-conjoined exact-match WHERE clauses? Should they support operators (gt, lt, like)? Should keys be mapped from camelCase to snake_case?
options: AND-conjoined exact-match WHERE clauses with camelCase→snake_case key mapping
Only exact-match, keys used as-is (caller responsible for snake_case)
Support simple operators via value convention (e.g., `{ 'created_at >': value }`)

ambiguities.description: `ValidationError` takes an optional `fieldErrors` map but the TypeScript type is unspecified. Could be `Record<string, string>`, `Record<string, string[]>`, or `Map<string, string[]>`. This affects how callers construct and consumers read field-level errors.
options: `Record<string, string>` — single error message per field
`Record<string, string[]>` — multiple error messages per field
